role_hints.oews_basis: 43-3031 Bookkeeping, Accounting, and Auditing Clerks 5.49% of the 4-digit 5412 wage bill — but 5412 is CPA-firm dominated (13-2011 Accountants 34.2%); within 541219 proper, bookkeeping staff are plausibly ~40-50% of wages (hypothesis)
role_hints.note: Bank-feed categorization, reconciliation and monthly close are already being automated natively inside QuickBooks/Xero — among the nearest-term fully automatable white-collar task sets.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-2011 Accountants and Auditors (non-CPA portion) + 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 5.1% at 4-digit; owner comp largely SDE outside wages; hypothesis ~25%
role_hints.note: Client relationships, cleanup judgment, IRS representation (EAs) and fractional-controller advice persist; the production share of their time is AI-exposed.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-3021 Billing and Posting Clerks 1.29% at 4-digit; a named sub-segment of this code; hypothesis ~10%
role_hints.note: Invoice generation, AR follow-up and statement processing are workflow-automation targets — also exposed to clients' own ERP/AI eliminating the outsourced function.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-2082 Tax Preparers 4.2% + payroll clerks in the 5412 tail; hypothesis ~10%
role_hints.note: Adjacent compliance tasks; overlaps 541213/541214 offerings at small firms.

source_hints.source: Intuit 10-K and investor materials (QuickBooks Live, Intuit Assist AI)
source_hints.why: The platform that owns the ledger publishing its own AI bookkeeping rollout — both adoption benchmark and the main capture threat.
Second-platform view of AI categorization/close automation adoption in the bookkeeper channel.
Practitioner-side AI adoption data covering non-CPA practices.

source_hints.source: NATP fee studies and NACPB (National Association of Certified Public Bookkeepers) surveys
source_hints.why: Fee benchmarks (monthly fixed fees, hourly rates) for bookkeeping and EA practices; verify NACPB's current survey output.

source_hints.source: Accounting Practice Sales and Poe Group Advisors (bookkeeping practice listings)
source_hints.why: Small-practice multiples — quoted on gross revenue with owner comp inside; normalize.

source_hints.source: NACPB / AIPB membership demographics; IRS enrolled-agent population data
source_hints.why: Owner-age evidence for a code with no licensure and many near-retirement solo operators; expect thin data.

capture_questions: Pricing is monthly fixed fees or hourly rates in a zero-licensure market with active offshore substitution: when platform AI cuts the hours behind a monthly close, does competition (offshore bureaus, QuickBooks Live's published price points, other local firms) force the fixed fee down to match, or do switching costs and trust let incumbent firms keep the spread as margin? Use QuickBooks Live pricing as the visible deflation benchmark.

Intuit owns the ledger: does QuickBooks Live plus native AI categorization convert bookkeeping clients away from independent firms, or convert firms into channel partners on Intuit's terms — who ends up owning the client relationship, the firm or the platform?
Is the client relationship personal to the owner-bookkeeper, and what fraction of a book survives an acquisition and owner exit — is there transferable enterprise value beyond the owner's labor?

What realistically stops an SMB from letting its accounting platform's built-in AI do the books entirely — is the residual product cleanup, oversight and liability comfort, and how much will clients pay for just that?
terminal_value_question: Transaction categorization, reconciliation and close are being automated inside the platforms themselves — the seller of the ledger is also the automator. In 5-10 years, does any paid human demand remain beyond cleanup of messy books, fractional-controller advisory and IRS representation (the EA niche) — and can a firm whose core product was the monthly close survive on those remnants? Distinguish AI cutting the firm's cost of producing the close from platform AI deleting the close as a purchasable service.
special_notes: Catch-all code — identify the top-3 revenue sub-segments, score the DOMINANT one, set heterogeneous: true. Hypothesis: (1) bookkeeping/write-up services (dominant), (2) billing and invoicing services, (3) other non-CPA accounting (enrolled agents, outsourced controller/CFO). Their moats differ: EA representation has a credential; bookkeeping has none.

No licensure moat at all — anyone may bookkeep; offshore substitution has already deflated pricing once, and platform AI is a second, steeper deflation on top. Easy to buy, weak capture: score C on evidence, not on the ease of acquisition.
Pricing-model deflation candidate: monthly fixed fees are benchmarked against visible platform prices (QuickBooks Live) — a published national price ceiling; model pi_dist against it explicitly.

Role-mix warning: the provided role_mix is 4-digit 5412 (CPA-dominated) and misstates this code; bookkeeping clerks and owner-operators dominate the real wage bill — use the hypothesis structure and flag it.

EBITDA normalization: overwhelmingly SDE businesses (35,754 of 45,390 firms under 5 employees); broker multiples are on gross revenue with owner comp inside — normalize before comparing. Note the record's n_band (531) sits oddly in the 100-499 and 500+ classes, where 'firms' may be outsourced-accounting/offshore-delivery companies rather than local bookkeepers — check what a 500+-employee 541219 firm actually is before treating it as an acquirable target.

Boundary confusion: CPA firms are 541211, tax prep storefronts 541213, payroll bureaus 541214; outsourced CFO/accounting companies with offshore delivery may also classify here — association surveys rarely align with the code, so check coverage before quoting.
